startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 35-year-old female executive presents with a 3-month history of weight loss (8 kg), tremor, heat intolerance, and palpitations. She appears anxious with a fine resting tremor and prominent eyes.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified Graves' disease based on the clinical features, confirmed with thyroid function tests showing elevated T3/T4 with suppressed TSH, and selected anti-thyroid medication as first-line management. The presence of thyroid-stimulating antibodies (TSI) binding to TSH receptors drives the hyperthyroidism in Graves' disease.

You identified some aspects correctly but missed key diagnostic or management steps. Review the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis and how thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins cause Graves' disease by mimicking TSH at the receptor level.

Review the endocrine physiology of the thyroid axis, including the role of the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system, T3/T4 synthesis from tyrosine residues requiring iodine, and the negative feedback loop involving TSH.

params.text:
Patient Presentation

A 35-year-old female executive visits your outpatient clinic with complaints of:

  • Unintentional weight loss of 8 kg over 3 months despite increased appetite
  • Tremor in both hands affecting her writing
  • Heat intolerance — she keeps the office AC at maximum
  • Palpitations, especially at night
  • Irregular menstrual periods

On examination:

  • HR: 110 bpm (regular), BP: 140/70 mmHg (wide pulse pressure)
  • Warm, moist skin with fine tremor of outstretched hands
  • Diffuse, non-tender thyroid enlargement (goitre)
  • Bilateral exophthalmos with lid retraction
  • Brisk reflexes throughout

You suspect a thyroid disorder. What investigation would you order first?

branchingQuestion.question: Which is the most appropriate initial investigation for this patient?

alternatives.text: Serum TSH and free T3/T4 levels

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: TSH is the most sensitive screening test for thyroid dysfunction. In hyperthyroidism, TSH is suppressed by negative feedback while free T3/T4 are elevated. The hypothalamus controls the anterior pituitary via releasing hormones transported through the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system.

feedback.title: Not the best first choice
feedback.subtitle: While ultrasound can assess thyroid morphology, it does not confirm the functional status. Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T3/T4) should always be the first investigation in suspected thyroid disease to establish whether the patient is hyper- or hypothyroid.

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The clinical features (weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance, exophthalmos, goitre) point strongly to hyperthyroidism, not adrenal pathology. Cortisol is secreted by the zona fasciculata of the adrenal cortex and is regulated by the HPA axis — a different endocrine axis entirely.

alternatives.text: Fasting blood glucose and insulin levels

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Although weight loss is present, the combination of goitre, exophthalmos, tremor, and heat intolerance strongly suggests hyperthyroidism, not a pancreatic disorder. Insulin is secreted by beta cells and promotes glucose uptake — this patient's presentation does not suggest insulin pathology.

params.text:
Investigation Results

The thyroid function tests return:

TestResultNormal Range
TSH0.01 mIU/L0.4–4.0 mIU/L
Free T458 pmol/L10–25 pmol/L
Free T318 pmol/L3.5–6.5 pmol/L

The results confirm primary hyperthyroidism — markedly elevated T3/T4 with suppressed TSH. Thyroid hormones T3 and T4 are synthesised from tyrosine residues on thyroglobulin and require iodine for their production.

Given her exophthalmos and diffuse goitre, Graves' disease is the most likely diagnosis. TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) are positive.

How would you manage this patient?

branchingQuestion.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for this patient with Graves' disease?

alternatives.text: Start carbimazole (anti-thyroid drug) + propranolol for symptom control

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Anti-thyroid drugs (carbimazole/methimazole) are the first-line treatment for Graves' disease. They inhibit thyroid peroxidase, blocking T3/T4 synthesis. A beta-blocker like propranolol provides rapid symptomatic relief of tremor, tachycardia, and anxiety while waiting for the anti-thyroid drug to take effect.

feedback.title: Not first-line
feedback.subtitle: Surgery is reserved for patients who fail medical therapy, have very large goitres, or have suspected malignancy. The patient must first be rendered euthyroid with anti-thyroid drugs before surgery to prevent thyroid storm. Medical management is always attempted first in uncomplicated Graves' disease.

feedback.title: Not the best initial approach
feedback.subtitle: Radioactive iodine is an effective definitive treatment but is usually considered after a trial of anti-thyroid drugs or when drugs are contraindicated. In a young woman of reproductive age, anti-thyroid drugs are preferred initially as RAI is contraindicated in pregnancy and requires contraception for 6 months post-treatment.

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Corticosteroids are not the primary treatment for Graves' hyperthyroidism. While they may be used for severe thyroid eye disease (Graves' ophthalmopathy), the hyperthyroidism itself requires anti-thyroid drugs. Cortisol is regulated by the HPA axis — a separate endocrine pathway.

params.text:
Management and Follow-up

You start the patient on:

  • Carbimazole 20 mg daily — blocks thyroid hormone synthesis by inhibiting thyroid peroxidase
  • Propranolol 40 mg TDS — controls tachycardia, tremor, and anxiety

Key Teaching Points:

  • In Graves' disease, thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) bind to TSH receptors, mimicking TSH action and causing unregulated thyroid hormone production
  • TSH is suppressed because elevated T3/T4 exert negative feedback on the anterior pituitary and hypothalamus via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system
  • The hypothalamus releases TRH, which stimulates TSH release from the anterior pituitary — this axis is suppressed in Graves' disease
  • Anti-thyroid drugs work by blocking the organification of iodine and coupling of iodotyrosines

The patient should be monitored with thyroid function tests every 4–6 weeks initially, with dose adjustment to maintain euthyroidism. Treatment typically continues for 12–18 months before considering drug withdrawal.
